title: Corrosion Resistance of Ru-Mo-W-Fe Alloy in Supercritical Acid and Electrochemical Measurement in Sulfuric Acid
author_string: Murugesan, Naveenkarthik, Murakami, Rikito, Yonemura, Kotaro, Itoi, Shiika, Kamada, Kei et al.
abstract:
Introduction
               
               Supercritical geothermal power generation is a next-generation geothermal power generation using geothermal fluid that exists at 5 km depth, which is deeper than conventional geothermal fluid, and is in supercritical state. The steam production capacity of supercritical geothermal fluid is estimated to be several times higher than that of conventional geothermal fluid. Though the environment is extremely corrosive that conventional corrosion resistance materials, such as stainless steels, Ni-based alloys or Ti alloys, cannot last for long-term. For a long-term producing, corrosion rate of materials used in supercritical geothermal environment should be less than 0.2 mm/y. There is a report that ceramics such as Al2O3 showed extremely good corrosion resistance (≈0.005 mm/y) in supercritical water[1]. Although, ceramic coatings on metals have often been failure due to a thermal expansion difference. In the other hand, noble metals such as Ir, Rh, Ru are also reported to have good corrosion resistance (<1 mm/y) in supercritical hydrochloric acid[2] and can be used as a coating without such failure. However noble metals are too expensive to use as a structural material (Al2O3 : 0.015 USD/g, Ir : 161 USD/g, Rh : 162 USD/g, Ru : 19 USD/g), Ru is relatively low price which might practically be used. Ru-Mo-W alloy was developed for resistive heating wire by our group[3]. Mo and W are added to improve workability and resistivity respectively. The tensile strength of the alloy is over 500 MPa, and breaking elongation is over 80%. The alloy can be used in 1,600 ℃ vacuum for 3,000 hours. Due to its fine workability and excellent heat resistance, we expected it to perform well in supercritical geothermal environment. In this study, Fe was added in the Ru-Mo-W alloy for cost efficiency and due to an expectation on the alloy to be used as a coating on ferrous substrate. To the Ru-Mo-W-Fe alloy, we evaluated its corrosion resistance in various types of acids including supercritical acids and conducted electrochemical measurements in 1 M H2SO4.
               
                  Experimental
               
               Ingots of Ru60(1-x)Mo15(1-x)W25(1-x)Fe100x(x=0.05, 0.1, 0.2) were prepared by arc melting method. Those ingots are formed into 2 mmØ rod by dewetting micro-pulling-down method. Those rod-shaped specimens are annealed at 1800 ℃ in high-purity Ar gas for 3 h. For an immersion test, those specimens were cut into 2 mmØ✕15 mm and immersed in H2SO4(300 ℃) for 24 h, 5 mass% HCl(25 ℃) for 72 h or 50 mass% HF(100 ℃) for 24 h. After immersions, mass loss was measured to quantify yearly corrosion rates. S31600 and S32750 stainless steels, N10276 Ni-based alloy and R56400 Ti alloy those cut into 10✕8✕t2 mm and mirror finished also immersed in those acids for a comparison. For a further understand of corrosion of Ru-based alloy, we conducted potentiodynamic anodic polarization in 1 M, H2SO4. We prepared plate specimens by stamping melts of Ru60(1-x)Mo15(1-x)W25(1-x)Fe100x(x=0.05, 0.1, 0.2) using copper stamp with a 15 mmØ✕2 mm blind hole carved, and used them for working electrode. The electrode surface was 1 cm2. Reference electrode was Ag/AgCl[sat. KCl]. Electrolyte was deareated with Ar gas advanced to the measurement.
               
                  Results
               
               Yearly corrosion rate of Ru60(1-x)Mo15(1-x)W25(1-x)Fe100x(x=0.05, 0.1, 0.2) in H2SO4(300 ℃) were less than that of comparison materials and fulfilled the goal of yearly corrosion rate, 0.2 m/y. Although the corrosion rate increased rapidly when the Fe concentration was 20 at%. The figure shows anodic polarization curves of Ru60(1-x)Mo15(1-x)W25(1-x)Fe100x(x=0.05, 0.1, 0.2) and Ru, Mo, W, Fe pure metals in 1 M H2SO4. It indicated that polarization behavior of Ru-Mo-W-Fe alloy is based on corrosion of Ru pure metal. The electrode surface of Ru-Mo-W-Fe alloy was covered with porous oxide layer that mainly consisted of tungsten oxide.
